claim: Blobless partial clone (git clone --filter=blob:none) is the recommended default strategy for repo analysis, offering 1.5-2x speed improvement over full clone with full commit history preserved.
evidence: Confirmed by D4 analysis pipeline findings with specific performance measurements; shallow clones impose 13-25x server CPU penalty.

claim: A 4-tier hybrid analysis pipeline (API pre-flight → blobless partial clone → conditional history clone → conditional full clone) is the optimal architecture for repo analysis skills.
evidence: D4 pipeline architecture with Phase 0-5 CLI command examples; supported by D7 API constraints and D2a-4 GitHub API capability matrix.

claim: GitHub API pre-flight checks can satisfy approximately 40% of analysis dimensions without any clone operation, using metadata, traffic, contributor, and code scanning endpoints.
evidence: D2a-4 GitHub API capability matrix covers 30+ endpoints; estimate based on dimension catalog from D1a and D1b.

claim: No single platform covers the full signal space for repo analysis; every major platform (SonarCloud, CodeScene, Codecov, Snyk, GitClear) has at least one significant blind spot.
evidence: D3a documented 7 universal blind spots across code quality platforms; D3b added 10 additional gaps specific to specialized platforms.

claim: Trivy supply chain attack (CVE-2026-33634, March 19 2026) compromised versions above v0.69.3; safe pin is trivy-action@v0.35.0 or Grype as an alternative.
evidence: D2b security tools findings with specific CVE number, date, and safe version pinning; this is CRITICAL operational security information.

claim: OpenSSF Scorecard API (api.securityscorecards.dev) provides pre-computed security scores for over 1 million public repositories at zero cost with no authentication required.
evidence: Confirmed across D2b, D3b, and D4 findings files; API confirmed operational at stated endpoint.

claim: Tree-sitter is the recommended universal AST parsing substrate for multi-language repo analysis, with 900+ parsers available under MIT license and active maintenance.
evidence: D2a-2 AST complexity tools analysis; used by GitHub Semantic, Neovim, Helix, Emacs; 24.4k GitHub stars.

claim: Hotspot analysis (code churn overlaid with cyclomatic complexity) is the highest-ROI single analysis dimension, identifying the files most likely to cause future bugs.
evidence: D1a dimension catalog rated hotspot analysis 5/5 signal value; D6 comparison frameworks confirm hotspot as primary DORA-correlated metric.

claim: SARIF 2.1.0 is the recommended output format for analysis findings, with deliberate absence of a native summary object and an underused rank field (0-100) that maps to confidence scores.
evidence: D5 output scoring analysis with SARIF structure deep-dive; rank field connection to TDMS confidence field noted as serendipitous discovery.

claim: The DORA framework added a 5th metric — Deployment Rework Rate — in October 2024, and the 2025 DORA report abandoned elite/high/medium/low tiers in favor of 7 team archetypes.
evidence: D6 comparison methodologies findings with specific dates; this represents a significant shift in how DORA metrics should be applied.

claim: GitHub App installation tokens expire in 1 hour (not 8 hours); the 8-hour figure applies only to user access tokens — a common confusion causing authentication failures.
evidence: D7 constraints and edge cases findings; confirmed as frequently-confused constraint with operational impact.

claim: GitHub Git Trees API caps at 100,000 entries and 7MB; when truncated:true appears in the response, directory structure analysis must fall back to alternative methods.
evidence: D7 API constraints; D2a-4 GitHub API capability matrix confirmed 100,000 entry limit with 7MB cap.

claim: Shallow clones impose a 13-25x CPU penalty on the GitHub server during subsequent fetch operations, making them unsuitable for any workflow requiring incremental updates.
evidence: D4 clone strategy analysis with specific performance multipliers; blobless clone recommended as alternative.

claim: GitHub stats API endpoints (contributor stats, commit activity, code frequency) fail silently or return 202 Accepted for repositories with 10,000 or more commits.
evidence: D7 constraints findings; D2a-4 rate limit and API failure taxonomy confirmed this boundary.

claim: AI code fingerprinting achieves 97.2% F1-score for detecting AI-generated commits using 53 features across 5 behavioral categories; Claude Code is identifiable by high conditional density (27.2%).
evidence: D11 competitive intelligence; cites arXiv 2601.17406 (2026) with specific metric values.

claim: Aider's repo-map pattern (tree-sitter + PageRank, ~1000 token budget, signatures only not bodies) is the recommended pattern for providing repo context to LLMs without exceeding context limits.
evidence: D12 AI-assisted analysis findings; confirmed as convergent pattern across multiple AI code analysis tools.

claim: LLMs suffer from 'lost in the middle' degradation with 85-95% recall at context boundaries but only 76-82% recall in the middle, requiring strategic content placement.
evidence: D12 AI-assisted analysis findings with specific recall percentages; critical constraint for any LLM-based repo analysis.

claim: Static analysis is 43-91% better at false positive reduction when combined with LLM synthesis in a hybrid architecture; pure LLM analysis has 12% FP rate vs 5% for static analysis.
evidence: D12 AI-assisted analysis comparison table with specific FP percentages and latency/cost metrics.

claim: RAG + AST chunking is the convergent architecture for AI-assisted code analysis, independently adopted by CodeRabbit, Cursor, Sourcegraph Cody, and Greptile.
evidence: D12 AI-assisted analysis findings; four independent platforms converged on same architecture without cross-pollination.

claim: SIG/Sigrid industry benchmarking covers 30,000+ systems; achieving 4-star rating correlates with 2x lower maintenance costs and 4x faster development velocity.
evidence: D6 comparison methodologies with specific business impact data from SIG research.

claim: The Community Sustainability Index formula is: CSI = 0.30·φ_commit + 0.25·φ_issue + 0.25·φ_PR + 0.20·φ_activity, empirically validated August 2025.
evidence: D6 comparison methodologies with formula derivation and validation date.

claim: 65% of popular GitHub repositories have a bus factor of 2 or lower, making bus factor analysis a high-signal project health indicator.
evidence: D1b project dimensions findings with specific percentage from empirical study.

claim: 86% of GitHub Actions workflows have no token permission limits set, representing a systemic supply chain security gap detectable via OpenSSF Scorecard.
evidence: D1b project dimensions findings; corroborated by D2b security tools OpenSSF Scorecard coverage.

claim: Sourcegraph became fully proprietary in August 2024 and eliminated its free tier in July 2025; it is no longer viable as a free or open-source code intelligence platform.
evidence: D3b specialized platforms findings with specific dates; resolves contradictions with older documentation describing Sourcegraph as open source.

claim: Trunk Check's web product was sunset in July 2025; the CLI toolchain remains available but the managed platform is gone.
evidence: D2a-1 static analyzers findings with specific sunset date.

claim: MegaLinter is licensed under AGPL-3.0, which may introduce license compliance concerns for commercial repo analysis products that redistribute or offer it as a service.
evidence: D2a-1 static analyzers findings with specific license flag.

claim: Plato (JavaScript complexity visualizer) was abandoned in 2014 and should not be used in any new analysis pipeline.
evidence: D2a-2 AST complexity tools findings with specific abandon date and warning flag.

claim: jscpd (duplicate code detection) implements MCP (Model Context Protocol) natively, enabling direct LLM tool use for copy-paste detection without wrapping.
evidence: D2a-2 AST complexity tools; serendipitous discovery of MCP integration.

claim: ESLint's lintText() API can analyze code without any filesystem access or clone operation, enabling pre-clone linting of individual files fetched via GitHub Contents API.
evidence: D2a-1 static analyzers serendipitous discovery; enables pre-clone analysis optimization.

claim: scc (Sloc Cloc and Code) includes a unique LOCOMO metric combining LOC, complexity, and test coverage into a single maintainability score unavailable in competing tools.
evidence: D2a-3 LOC/churn/architecture tools findings; LOCOMO metric identified as differentiating capability.

claim: No analysis platform connects code churn, security vulnerability severity, community health, and dependency risk into a single cross-dimension correlation view.
evidence: D3b specialized platforms gap analysis confirmed 10 documented gaps; D3a universal blind spots corroborate.

claim: GitHub GraphQL API exposes branchProtectionRules, securityAndAnalysis, discussions, and packages data that is unavailable via REST API endpoints.
evidence: D2a-4 GitHub API capability matrix with specific GraphQL-only field identification.

claim: Socket.dev's reachability analysis eliminates approximately 80% of CVEs as unreachable in practice, dramatically reducing false positives in dependency vulnerability scanning.
evidence: D2b security tools findings; D3b specialized platforms corroboration of reachability analysis impact.

claim: deps.dev API covers 50 million+ packages with no authentication required, making it a zero-cost complement to Snyk and Socket.dev for dependency analysis.
evidence: D2b security tools findings with package count and authentication requirements confirmed.

claim: A proposed 6-dimension radar output (Security, Reliability, Maintainability, Documentation, Process, Velocity) with 4-artifact delivery (analysis.json, findings.jsonl, trends.jsonl, summary.md) represents the optimal output design for repo analysis.
evidence: D5 output scoring synthesis; proposed design, not yet validated against user needs or existing tooling.

claim: Scoring bands for repo health should be: 0-39 Critical (Red), 40-59 Needs Work (Orange), 60-79 Healthy (Yellow), 80-100 Excellent (Green), aligned with SonarQube A-E tiers.
evidence: D5 output scoring recommendations; derived from SonarQube and SIG benchmarking patterns.

claim: GitHub imposed a 100,000-repository ownership limit on April 28 2025, and added undisclosed GraphQL execution-resource limits in September 2025.
evidence: D7 constraints and edge cases with specific dates; the GraphQL limits are described as 'undisclosed' — their exact thresholds are unknown.

claim: LFX Insights relaunched in May 2025 covering 15,000+ repositories with Health Scores across 4 dimensions; it is free for Linux Foundation projects and increasingly accessible for external use.
evidence: D11 competitive intelligence findings; relaunch confirmed but external accessibility details uncertain.

claim: golangci-lint v2 introduced a breaking change removing the --out-format flag; analysis pipelines using golangci-lint must use --format instead.
evidence: D2a-1 static analyzers findings with specific breaking change identification.

claim: TDMS-compatible JSONL schema for findings output enables direct intake into existing debt management infrastructure without transformation layer.
evidence: D5 output scoring findings connecting SARIF rank field to TDMS confidence field; direct compatibility confirmed.

claim: Quick Scan (Phase 0 API-only, no clone) is the default mode of the repo analysis skill. It delivers 40-55% of full analysis signal in under 30 seconds using 8-10 API calls.
evidence: G1 gap analysis confirmed Phase 0 delivers 24 of 36 dimensions at least partially; highest-signal security and governance dimensions are API-satisfiable. Contrarian Challenge 4 identified this gap in the original design.

claim: The DORA 5th metric is Rework Rate (ratio of unplanned deployments caused by production bugs), not Reliability. Reliability was added in 2021 as a quasi-metric but is not counted in the official 5.
evidence: V1 verification confirmed this via CD Foundation blog post October 16, 2025. The original synthesis incorrectly named the 5th metric.

claim: GitHub API coverage for repo analysis is 40-55% (partial signal) or 40% (full satisfaction) depending on definition. The contrarian's 28-33% estimate is too low; the original 40% claim is defensible for full-satisfaction coverage.
evidence: G1 cross-referenced 36 analysis dimensions against verified API endpoints. 24 of 36 partially satisfiable; 18-20 fully satisfiable without caveats. The 40% figure is defensible as full-coverage estimate.

claim: Scoring should use categorical bands (Critical/Needs Work/Healthy/Excellent) as the primary display, not false-precision numeric composites. Scores have ±10-15 point confidence intervals given tool FP rates.
evidence: Contrarian Challenge 3 demonstrated that composite numeric scores conceal measurement uncertainty. Tool FP rates (5-12%) and scoring formula choices dramatically change outputs. Bands are the honest display format.

claim: The skill count is 65 (not 62) and agent count is 57 (not 39). The discrepancy in agent count is explained by the .claude/agents/global/ subdirectory containing 13 additional agents not counted in the original synthesis.
evidence: V2 internal verification via filesystem ls commands. 65 skill directories confirmed. 44 agents in .claude/agents/ + 13 in .claude/agents/global/ = 57 total.

claim: Trivy supply chain attack (CVE-2026-33634) was more extensive than initially reported: 76 of 77 trivy-action tags were force-pushed to malware, all 7 setup-trivy tags were replaced, and Google mirror.gcr.io continued serving malicious v0.69.6 after Docker Hub cleanup.
evidence: V1 verification against official Trivy advisory, Microsoft Security Blog, and maintainer discussion. The mirror.gcr.io hazard is critical for Kubernetes environments using containerd defaults.

claim: 7 named absence patterns form a classifier that detects anti-pattern clusters: Ghost Ship, Test Theater, Security Facade, Borrowed Armor, Dependency Freeze, Lone Wolf, Silent Failure. Each has a specific detection logic and severity.
evidence: G2 gap analysis synthesized patterns from OSSF Scorecard, GitHub Community Health API, and the brief tool detection pipeline. Pattern names and definitions are novel to this skill design.

claim: A 5-signal temporal fingerprint (commit velocity, contributor churn, test-to-code ratio trajectory, dependency file touch frequency, churn vs net growth) can be extracted with pure git log commands in under 30 seconds for repos under 10,000 commits.
evidence: G2 gap analysis with verified git commands and performance benchmarks. Sparkline output using Unicode block characters requires no external dependencies.

claim: CodeRabbit and Greptile solve ongoing PR review (own codebase), not point-in-time external repo evaluation. The custom skill's economic justification is external evaluation + SoNash workflow integration + custom scoring — not ongoing PR review.
evidence: G1 make-vs-buy analysis. CodeRabbit at $24/dev/month for a solo operator costs $288/year and cannot be replicated by a custom skill for that cost. But CodeRabbit requires installing as GitHub App on connected repos and produces PR comments, not structured health reports.

claim: Star velocity, fork-to-star ratio, and watcher-to-star ratio are more diagnostic signals than raw star count. Fork-to-star > 0.15 indicates active building; watcher-to-star > 0.05 indicates operational criticality.
evidence: OTB-1 outside-the-box analysis. Thresholds are estimates requiring calibration against real repos. The four star curve shapes (spike-and-decay, steady ramp, staircase, cliff) are named patterns with distinct interpretations.
